subject: Physics
q: Two harmonic waves moving in the same direction superimpose to form a wave $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a} \cos (1.5 \mathrm{t}) \cos (50.5 \mathrm{t})$ where $t$ is in seconds. Find the period with which they beat (close to nearest integer)
question: Two harmonic waves moving in the same direction superimpose to form a wave $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a} \cos (1.5 \mathrm{t}) \cos (50.5 \mathrm{t})$ where $t$ is in seconds. Find the period with which they beat (close to nearest integer)
solution:
The given equation can be written as
$\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \cos [1.5+50.5] \mathrm{t}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \cos [50.5-1.5]$
$x=\frac{a}{2} \cos [52 t]+\frac{a}{2} \cos [49 t]$
Here, $2 \pi \mathrm{f}_1 \& 2 \pi \mathrm{f}_2=49$
$\mathrm{f}_1=\frac{52}{2 \pi}, \mathrm{f}_2=\frac{49}{2 \pi}$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}_{\text {Bat }}=\mathrm{f}_1-\mathrm{f}_2=\frac{3}{2 \pi} \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}_{\text {Beat }}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\text {Beat }}}=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \mathrm{sec}$
$=2.09 \mathrm{sec} \approx 2 \mathrm{sec}$

subject: Physics
q: A granite rod of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length is clamped at its middle point and is set into longitudinal vibrations. The density of granite is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and its Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9.27</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi>Pa</mi></math>. What will be the fundamental frequency of the longitudinal vibrations?
question: A granite rod of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length is clamped at its middle point and is set into longitudinal vibrations. The density of granite is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and its Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9.27</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi>Pa</mi></math>. What will be the fundamental frequency of the longitudinal vibrations?
solution:
L=⋋2⇒⋋=2L=1.2 m

v=yρ=5.86×103 m s-1

∴ Fundamental frequency=v⋋=4.88 kHz

≈5 kHz

subject: Physics
q: A closed organ pipe 150 cm long gives 7 beats per second with an open organ pipe of length 350 cm, both vibrating in fundamental mode. The velocity of sound is ______ m s-1.
question: A closed organ pipe 150 cm long gives 7 beats per second with an open organ pipe of length 350 cm, both vibrating in fundamental mode. The velocity of sound is ______ m s-1.
solution:
The formula to calculate the fundamental frequency in a closed pipe is given by

fc=v4l1   ...1

And, that in an open pipe is given by

fo=v2l2   ...2

Given that

fc-fo=7   ...3

From equations (1), (2) and (3), it follows that

v4×150-v2×350=7⇒v600 cm-v700 cm=7⇒v6m-v7m=7⇒v142=7⇒v=42×7=294 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A wire having a linear mass density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is stretched between two rigid supports with a tension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The wire resonates at a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The next higher frequency at which the same wire resonates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>550</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The length of the wire is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A wire having a linear mass density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is stretched between two rigid supports with a tension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The wire resonates at a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The next higher frequency at which the same wire resonates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>550</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The length of the wire is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
fn=nv2ℓ=500
fn+1=(n+1)v2ℓ=550
n+1n=1110
Thus, n=10
Thus, ℓ=nv2fnv=Tμ
=102×500×9009×10-4=10 m

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> and mass per unit length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6.0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is put under tension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>540</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Two consecutive frequencies that it resonates at are: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>420</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>490</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math><mi>L</mi></math> in meters is :
question: A wire of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> and mass per unit length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6.0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is put under tension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>540</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Two consecutive frequencies that it resonates at are: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>420</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>490</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math><mi>L</mi></math> in meters is :
solution:
Fundamental frequency =490-420=70 Hz
70=12LTμ
⇒70=12L5406×10-3
⇒L=12×7090×103=300140
⇒L≈2.14 m

q: Three sound waves of equal amplitudes have frequencies $(v-1), v,(v+1)$. They superpose to give beats. The number of beats produced per second will be
question: Three sound waves of equal amplitudes have frequencies $(v-1), v,(v+1)$. They superpose to give beats. The number of beats produced per second will be
solution:
$\quad(3)$
Maximum number of beats $=v+1-(v-1)=2$

subject: Physics
q: A closed organ and an open organ tube are filled by two different gases having same bulk modulus but different densities $\rho_1$ and $\rho_{2^{\prime}}$, respectively. The frequency of $9^{\text {th }}$ harmonic of closed tube is identical with $4^{\text {th }}$ harmonic of open tube. If the length of the closed tube is 10 cm and the density ratio of the gases is $\rho_1: \rho_2=1: 16$, then the length of the open tube is :
question: A closed organ and an open organ tube are filled by two different gases having same bulk modulus but different densities $\rho_1$ and $\rho_{2^{\prime}}$, respectively. The frequency of $9^{\text {th }}$ harmonic of closed tube is identical with $4^{\text {th }}$ harmonic of open tube. If the length of the closed tube is 10 cm and the density ratio of the gases is $\rho_1: \rho_2=1: 16$, then the length of the open tube is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 9^{\text {th }} \text { harmonic of closed pipe }=\frac{9 \mathrm{~V}_1}{4 \ell_1} \\ & 4^{\text {th }} \text { harmonic of open pipe }=\frac{2 \mathrm{~V}_2}{\ell_2} \\ & \therefore \frac{9 \mathrm{~V}_1}{4 \ell_1}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~V}_2}{\ell_2} \\ & \therefore \frac{9}{4 \ell_1} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~B}}{\rho_1}}=\frac{2}{\ell_2} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~B}}{\rho_2}} \Rightarrow \frac{\ell_2}{\ell_1}=\frac{8}{9} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}} \\ & \ell_2=\ell_1 \times \frac{8}{9} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{20}{9} \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is stretched between two clamps <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart. The extension developed in the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the fundamental frequency of vibration in the wire will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math>
question: A wire of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is stretched between two clamps <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart. The extension developed in the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the fundamental frequency of vibration in the wire will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math>
solution:
Using the relation of Young's modulus,

 TA=YΔLL  
⇒T=YΔLL×A

The linear mass density is  μ=mL.

So,
Tμ=YΔLALmL=Y(ΔL)×LAL(m)=YΔLL×1ρ

Substituting the values,
Tμ=8×1010×3.2×10-40.5×18×103=6.4×103
⇒Tμ=64×102

The fundamental frequency is given by f=12LTμ.
⇒Tμ=8×10=80 m s-1
Therefore,

f=801=80 Hz

subject: Physics
q: A travelling harmonic wave is represented by the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>50</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mtext>,</mtext></math> where <math><mi>x</mi></math> and <math><mi>y</mi></math> are in meter and <math><mi>t</mi></math> is in seconds. Which of the following is a correct statement about the wave?
question: A travelling harmonic wave is represented by the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>50</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mtext>,</mtext></math> where <math><mi>x</mi></math> and <math><mi>y</mi></math> are in meter and <math><mi>t</mi></math> is in seconds. Which of the following is a correct statement about the wave?
options: The wave is propagating along the positive <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>

The wave is propagating along the positive <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>

The wave is propagating along the negative <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>

The wave is propagating along the negative <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>
solution:
Given wave equation, yx, t=10-3sin50t+2x

This equation is similar to the equation of wave travelling along the negative x-axis which is given by

yx,t=Asinωt+kx

After comparing, Angular frequency ω=50 s-1

Wave number k=2 m-1

The wave velocity, v=ωk=502=25 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A train is moving on a straight track with speed 20 m s-1 . It is blowing its whistle at the frequency of 1000 Hz. The percentage change in the frequency heard by a person standing near the track as the train passes him is (speed of sound =320 m s-1 ) close to:
question: A train is moving on a straight track with speed 20 m s-1 . It is blowing its whistle at the frequency of 1000 Hz. The percentage change in the frequency heard by a person standing near the track as the train passes him is (speed of sound =320 m s-1 ) close to:
solution:
Before train passes

f′=vv-vs×f0

=320320-20×f0

=320300×f0

=16f015

After train passes

f′′=vv+vs×f0

f′′=320320+20f0

=320340f0

=1617f0

Change in frequency

=1615-1617f0

∴ Percentage change in frequency

=1615-1617f0f0×100 ≈12% nearly

subject: Physics
q: Two factories are sounding their sirens at 800 $\mathrm{Hz}$. A man goes from one factory to other at a speed of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The velocity of sound is $320 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The number of beats heard by the person in one second will be:
question: Two factories are sounding their sirens at 800 $\mathrm{Hz}$. A man goes from one factory to other at a speed of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The velocity of sound is $320 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The number of beats heard by the person in one second will be:
solution:
Given: Frequency of sound produced by siren, $f=800 \mathrm{~Hz}$
Speed of observer, $u=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Velocity of sound, $v=320 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
No. of beats heard per second $=$ ?
No. of extra waves received by the observer per second $=\pm 4 \lambda$
$\therefore \quad$ No. of beats/ sec
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\frac{2}{\lambda}-\left(-\frac{2}{\lambda}\right)=\frac{4}{\lambda} \\
&=\frac{2 \times 2}{\frac{320}{800}} \quad\left(\because \lambda=\frac{V}{f}\right) \\
&=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 800}{320}=10
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A resonance tube is old and has a jagged end. It is still used in the laboratory to determine the velocity of sound in air. A tuning fork of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>512</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Hz</mi></math> produces first resonance when the tube is filled with water to a mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> below a reference mark, near the open end of the tube. The experiment is repeated with another fork of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>256</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Hz</mi></math> which produces first resonance when water reaches a mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> below the reference mark. The velocity of sound in air, obtained in the experiment, is close to
question: A resonance tube is old and has a jagged end. It is still used in the laboratory to determine the velocity of sound in air. A tuning fork of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>512</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Hz</mi></math> produces first resonance when the tube is filled with water to a mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> below a reference mark, near the open end of the tube. The experiment is repeated with another fork of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>256</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Hz</mi></math> which produces first resonance when water reaches a mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> below the reference mark. The velocity of sound in air, obtained in the experiment, is close to
solution:
λ14=11+e ⇒v512 × 4=11+e    ...(1)

λ24=27+e ⇒v256 × 4=27+e    ...(2)

Subtracting the equations,

v256×4×12=16 cm=0.16 m

⇒v=0.16×2×4×256

         =328 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment with sonometer when a mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is attached to the string, it vibrates with fundamental frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. When a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is attached, the string vibrates with fundamental frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>.
question: In an experiment with sonometer when a mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is attached to the string, it vibrates with fundamental frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. When a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is attached, the string vibrates with fundamental frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>.
solution:
The formula to calculate the fundamental frequency in a stretched string is given by

f=12LTμ   ...1

For the first mass, it can be written that

f1=12Lm1gμ   ...2

And, for the second mass, it can be written that

f2=12Lm2gμ   ...3

Divide equation (3) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required mass.

f2f1=12Lm2gμ12Lm1gμ= m2m1⇒m1m2=f1f22⇒m2=m1f2f12   ...4

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (4) to calculate the required mass.

m2=180 g×50 Hz30 Hz2= 500 g

subject: Physics
q: The speed of a transverse wave passing through a string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The area of cross-section of the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and its Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The extension of the wire over its natural length due to its tension will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: The speed of a transverse wave passing through a string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The area of cross-section of the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and its Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The extension of the wire over its natural length due to its tension will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
The wave speed in string is given by vw=Tμ, where, T is tension and μ=ml is linear mass density.

Putting the values, we have

⇒60=T10×10-3×0.5

⇒T=602×10-20.5=72 N

The Young's modulus is given by Y=FlA∆l, here, A is area.

From above the extension of the wire is Δl=TlAY=72×0.52×10-6×1.2×1011=72×524×10-5=15×10-5 m.

Hence, the value of x=15.

question:

(Take, g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Velocity in a string, at a distance x, is given by

v=µgxµ=gx

⇒ dx dt = gx

⇒∫020dxx=g∫0tdt

On integrating, we get, 

t=22  s

q: The engine of a train moving with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards a platform sounds a whistle at frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The frequency heard by a passenger inside the train is: (Neglect air speed. Speed of sound in air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>330</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: The engine of a train moving with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards a platform sounds a whistle at frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The frequency heard by a passenger inside the train is: (Neglect air speed. Speed of sound in air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>330</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
The formula to calculate the frequency of the sound as heard by the passenger inside the train can be written as

f'=f0v-vOv-vs   ...1

Since the passenger is inside the train, the speeds of the source and the observer are the same.

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to get the required frequency.

f'=400330-10330-10 Hz=400 Hz

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as
Reason $\mathbf{R}$
Assertion A: A sound wave has higher speed in solids than gases.
Reason R: Gases have higher value of Bulk modulus than solids.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as
Reason $\mathbf{R}$
Assertion A: A sound wave has higher speed in solids than gases.
Reason R: Gases have higher value of Bulk modulus than solids.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true but $\mathbf{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$
Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true and $\mathbf{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$
solution:
Speed of sound in a medium depends on inertial and elastic properties as $v=\sqrt{\frac{B}{P}}$ for gases and $v=\sqrt{\frac{Y}{P}}$ for solids. Since the elastic property of solid happens to be many folds greater than that of gases, the speed of sound in solids i5 higher than in gases.

Also, bulk modulus of gases varies between 0 and $\infty\left(B=-v \frac{d P}{d v}\right)$ hence reason is false.

subject: Physics
q: The equation of a plane progressive wave is given by $y = 5\cos\pi\left(200t - \dfrac{x}{150}\right)$ where $x$ and $y$ are in cm and $t$ is in second. The velocity of the wave is _______ m/s.
question: The equation of a plane progressive wave is given by $y = 5\cos\pi\left(200t - \dfrac{x}{150}\right)$ where $x$ and $y$ are in cm and $t$ is in second. The velocity of the wave is _______ m/s.
solution:
The given equation of the wave is $y = 5\cos\pi\left(200t - \dfrac{x}{150}\right)$

Rewriting the equation, we get $y = 5\cos\left(200\pi t - \dfrac{\pi x}{150}\right)$

Comparing this with the standard wave equation $y = A\cos(\omega t - kx)$, we have:

$\omega = 200\pi$ rad/s

$k = \dfrac{\pi}{150}$ rad/cm

The velocity of the wave is given by $v = \dfrac{\omega}{k}$

$v = \dfrac{200\pi}{\dfrac{\pi}{150}} = 30000$ cm/s

Converting to m/s, we get $v = \dfrac{30000}{100} = 300$ m/s

Answer: $300$

Assuming the speed of sound in air at STP is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the frequency difference between the fundamental and second harmonic of this pipe is __________ Hz.

subject: Physics
q: A point source is emitting sound waves of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the origin. The difference in intensity (magnitude only) at two points located at a distances of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the origin respectively will be ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A point source is emitting sound waves of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the origin. The difference in intensity (magnitude only) at two points located at a distances of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the origin respectively will be ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Intensity for a point source varies as I∝1r2.

Intensity at a distance of 2 m, I2=16×10-8×122=4×10-8 W m-2

Similarly, intensity at a distance of 4 m, I4=16×10-8×142=1×10-8 W m-2

Required difference, I2-I4=3×10-8 W m-2

q: Two strings with circular cross section and made of same material, are stretched to have same amount of tension. A transverse wave is then made to pass through both the strings. The velocity of the wave in the first string having the radius of cross section R is $\mathrm{v}_1$, and that in the other string having radius of cross section $R / 2$ is $v_2$. Then $\frac{v_2}{v_1}=$
question: Two strings with circular cross section and made of same material, are stretched to have same amount of tension. A transverse wave is then made to pass through both the strings. The velocity of the wave in the first string having the radius of cross section R is $\mathrm{v}_1$, and that in the other string having radius of cross section $R / 2$ is $v_2$. Then $\frac{v_2}{v_1}=$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\frac{T}{f \pi R^2}} \\ & \frac{v_2}{v_1}=\frac{R_1}{R_2}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two loudspeakers ($L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$) are placed with a separation of 10 m, as shown in figure. Both speakers are fed with an audio input signal of same frequency with constant volume. A voice recorder, initially at point $A$, at equidistance to both loud speakers, is moved by 25 m along the line $A B$ while monitoring the audio signal. The measured signal was found to undergo 10 cycles of minima and maxima during the movement. The frequency of the input signal is $\_\_\_\_$ Hz (Speed of sound in air is $324 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\sqrt{5}=2.23$)
question: Two loudspeakers ($L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$) are placed with a separation of 10 m, as shown in figure. Both speakers are fed with an audio input signal of same frequency with constant volume. A voice recorder, initially at point $A$, at equidistance to both loud speakers, is moved by 25 m along the line $A B$ while monitoring the audio signal. The measured signal was found to undergo 10 cycles of minima and maxima during the movement. The frequency of the input signal is $\_\_\_\_$ Hz (Speed of sound in air is $324 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\sqrt{5}=2.23$)
solution:
Point B will $10^{\text {th }}$ maxima
$\Delta x=L_2 B-L_1 B$
$L_1 B=\sqrt{20^2+40^2}=20 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~m}=44.6 \mathrm{~m}$
$L_2 B=\sqrt{40^2+30^2}=50 \mathrm{~m}$
$\Delta x=50-44.6=5.4 \mathrm{~m}$
$\Delta x=n \lambda$
$5.4=10 \times \lambda$
$\lambda=0.54 \mathrm{~m}$
$V=f \lambda$
$f=\frac{324}{0.54}=600 \mathrm{~Hz}$

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: In the resonance tube experiment, if the tuning fork is replaced by another identical turning fork but with its arm having been filled, the length of the air column should be increased to obtain resonance again.
Statement 2: On filling the arms, the frequency of a tuning fork increases.
question:
This question has Statement 1and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: In the resonance tube experiment, if the tuning fork is replaced by another identical turning fork but with its arm having been filled, the length of the air column should be increased to obtain resonance again.
Statement 2: On filling the arms, the frequency of a tuning fork increases.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is a correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution:
On loading frequency of a tuning fork decreases and on filing frequency increases.
If a tuning fork filled length $x$, then new frequency produced by the tuning fork $n^{\prime}=n+x$

subject: Physics
q: The fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe is equal to the first overtone frequency of an open organ pipe. If length of the open pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, the length of the closed pipe will be :
question: The fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe is equal to the first overtone frequency of an open organ pipe. If length of the open pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, the length of the closed pipe will be :
solution:
λ4=L1                                2λ2=L2

As, v=fλ                         ⇒f2=2v2L2

⇒v=f14L1                 ⇒f2=vL2

⇒f1=v4L1

As per question, f1=f2

v4L1=vL2

⇒L2=4L1

⇒60=4×L1

⇒L1=15 cm

subject: Physics
q:
A tunning fork of frequency 340 Hz resonates in the fundamental mode with an air column of length 125 cm in a cylindrical tube closed at one end. When water is slowly poured in it, the minimum height of water required for observing resonance once again is _____ cm.
(Velocity of sound in air is 340 ms-1)
question:
A tunning fork of frequency 340 Hz resonates in the fundamental mode with an air column of length 125 cm in a cylindrical tube closed at one end. When water is slowly poured in it, the minimum height of water required for observing resonance once again is _____ cm.
(Velocity of sound in air is 340 ms-1)
solution:
We know that, v=λf, then, λ=vf=340 m s-1340 Hz=340 m s-1340 s-1=1 m

First resonating length, l1=λ4=1004=25 cm

Second resonating length, l2=3λ4=3×1004=75 cm

Third resonating length, l3=5λ4=5×1004=125 cm

So third resonance is not possible since the length of the tube is 125 cm

Thus, minimum height of water necessary for resonance =125−75=50 cm

subject: Physics
q: Two travelling waves of equal amplitudes and equal frequencies move in opposite directions along a string. They interfere to produce a stationary wave whose equation is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>cos</mi><mi>π</mi><mi>x</mi><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math>. The amplitude of the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: Two travelling waves of equal amplitudes and equal frequencies move in opposite directions along a string. They interfere to produce a stationary wave whose equation is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>cos</mi><mi>π</mi><mi>x</mi><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math>. The amplitude of the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Amplitude is the maximum displacement from the mean position, for that

sin2πtT=1

Therefore, A=10cos43π=10×12= 5 cm

subject: Physics
q: A string is stretched between fixed points separated by $75 \mathrm{~cm}$. It is observed to have resonant frequencies of $420 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and $315 \mathrm{~Hz}$. There are no other resonant frequencies between these two. Then, the lowest resonant frequency for this string is
question: A string is stretched between fixed points separated by $75 \mathrm{~cm}$. It is observed to have resonant frequencies of $420 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and $315 \mathrm{~Hz}$. There are no other resonant frequencies between these two. Then, the lowest resonant frequency for this string is
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2 \ell}(\mathrm{v})=315, \frac{(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2 \ell} \mathrm{v}=420$
Solving $\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 \ell}=105$
